title: Service of Court Documents by a Bailiff – Rules and Procedure in Poland
description: Learn how a court bailiff serves legal correspondence in Poland. When is bailiff service required, how to place an order, and what are the costs?
content:
What Is Bailiff Service of Documents?

Bailiff service of documents is a procedure in which a court bailiff (komornik sądowy) personally delivers court correspondence to the addressee. This mechanism gained particular importance following the 2019 amendment to the Code of Civil Procedure, which introduced an obligation for parties to attempt bailiff service when the first postal delivery is returned uncollected.

Legal Basis

Bailiff service is regulated by Art. 3(4) of the Act on Court Bailiffs and Art. 1391 of the Code of Civil Procedure, introduced by the amendment of 4 July 2019. This provision requires civil parties to attempt service via a bailiff when the respondent failed to collect a court document sent by post (returned with a delivery notice).

When Is Bailiff Service Used?

Bailiff service is used when:

  • a court document (typically a copy of a claim or payment order) was returned twice undelivered by post,
  • a party has difficulty establishing the addressee's current address,
  • the court orders the bailiff to serve a document within its jurisdiction,
  • a party wants verified confirmation that the addressee actually received the document.

How to Commission Service?

A service commission may be filed by a party to the proceedings (claimant or defendant), a legal representative (advocate or attorney), or by the court ex officio. The commission must include a copy of the document to be served and the addressee's details. The Bailiff's Office No. IX in Otwock accepts commissions in person, by post, or electronically.

The Service Procedure

The bailiff attempts personal delivery at the stated address. They may hand the document directly to the addressee, leave it with an adult household member or concierge, or establish a new address by querying population registers (PESEL, CEPIK, local authority records). A protocol is drawn up for every service attempt, which can be submitted to the court as evidence.

Consequences of Unsuccessful Service

If the bailiff is unable to effect delivery and establishes that the addressee does not reside at the stated address, the court may deem service complete (constructive service). The bailiff's protocol is then key evidence in the proceedings.

Costs

The fee for bailiff service commissioned by a party is PLN 60 gross per document, regardless of distance. The commissioning party initially bears the cost but may subsequently recover it from the losing party as part of the court costs.
